Recruiting is one of the most important jobs in the economy.

Every great company gets built by the people its founders chose to hire. 

Every meaningful career has a recruiter behind it somewhere. The one who picked up the phone, made the case, built the relationship over years.

The tools the industry has been given don't act like that's true.

For 15 years, recruiting CRMs have turned a relationship craft into a data-entry job. Source. Tag. Filter. Update the pipeline. Write the email. Update the pipeline again. £150/user/month for software built for 500-person staffing groups, sold to 5-person UK agencies.

We started @Shorltists because one solo recruiter inside Bifrost Studios - our Copenhagen venture studio - was paying enterprise prices for a CRM built for an enterprise she wasn't part of. We dug in and realised the same problem hits 95% of UK recruitment.

So we partnered with the recruiters we trusted, and built the tool they wished existed.

Shortlists is the recruiting CRM, ATS, and BD Radar built for 2-to-10 person UK agencies.

→ One tool, not five. Replaces your CRM, ATS, BD tracker, AI notetaker, and client portal. 
→ £62/user/month. No annual contract. No hidden add-ons. 
→ BD Radar reads every recruiting board, funding round, and leadership change overnight - and surfaces fee-earning roles before they're posted publicly. 
→ Free CTO-led migration from Bullhorn / Vincere / Loxo / Atlas - overnight, before your next working day starts.

The job-to-be-done: get to the hiring manager before the job hits LinkedIn.
When AI takes the admin layer, the craft is what's left. The next generation of recruiting CRMs will be the ones that respect that.

If you're running a 2-to-10 person UK agency, DM me.

7-Eleven just automated 95 percent of its hiring process through Workday's AI agents. Not assisted. Not accelerated. Automated.

Workday's Innovation Summit last week was full of numbers like this. 75 percent of their 11,500 customers are using Workday AI in core processes. NetApp cut 24,000 contracts down to 600 in days. These are not pilots. These are production results.

What the coverage keeps glossing over: the customers getting these results are not the ones who bought the best AI feature. They're the ones who did the organisational work first. Every customer panel at the Summit circled back to the same three blockers — data quality, process clarity, and culture. The AI didn't solve those. It rewarded the companies that already had.

That's the uncomfortable implication for mid-market leaders watching from the sidelines. Workday's agentic architecture is moving fast. But if your HR or finance processes are messy, your data is inconsistent, and your teams haven't agreed on what good looks like — an agent will automate the mess, not fix it. You won't get a dramatic reduction in payroll query handling time. You'll get faster errors.

The companies getting results right now didn't find a better tool. They changed something structurally before they switched anything on.

What would your processes look like if an agent had to execute them tomorrow, without a human to paper over the gaps?

Follow Metis Point — technology strategy without the big-firm overhead.
Source: diginomica

#AIStrategy #AIAdoption #BusinessAI #UKTech #Technology

We're hiring → Contracts & Legal Associate
📍 Cairo, Egypt  |  Full-Time  |  On-Site

At Nsas Travel, we operate NDC-X — a B2B distribution platform connecting airlines, GDS systems, and travel agencies across the region. Our contracts portfolio spans GDS agreements, airline incentive deals, GSA arrangements, and API distribution contracts.

We move fast. We work on high-value commercial agreements. And we need someone who can keep up.

─────────────────────────

What you'll do:

◆ Review, draft & redline commercial agreements
◆ Track contract obligations, deadlines & performance thresholds — nothing falls through the cracks
◆ Support live negotiations with briefing notes and term summaries
◆ Own the team task tracker and follow up across internal & external stakeholders
◆ Use AI tools daily to accelerate contract review, clause comparison & document drafting

─────────────────────────

What we're looking for:

✔ Law degree
✔ 1–2 years in contracts, legal, or commercial operations
✔ Strong written English — you'll draft and communicate in English daily
✔ Advanced proficiency in Microsoft Word & Excel — you will use both every single day
✔ Genuine, daily AI tool user  — non-negotiable
✔ Aviation or travel industry background is a plus, not a requirement

─────────────────────────

What sets the right candidate apart:

You don't wait to be told something is late.
You build systems so nothing is ever late.
You think legally and act commercially.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗡𝗥𝗥 𝗖𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆

A 105% NRR can be a green flag. Or it can be a cover story.

Same average. Different company.

Most PE-backed software businesses are reporting the first and living in the second.

What's driving it isn't cyclical.

The COVID hiring surge has fully unwound. Fewer people means fewer seats. Roles that don't come back don't renew.

AI is compounding it. Quietly. Teams that needed twelve analysts now need eight. The work still gets done. The licenses don't get renewed.

And procurement reset the default. Not just cuts, the buying behaviour changed. Audit first. Renew only what's demonstrably used. That doesn't reverse when budgets recover.

The headcount model shifted too. Where hiring has come back, teams are leaner and more automated. The seat count no longer follows headcount the way it used to.

All of this gets compressed into one NRR number that looks stable right up until a buyer breaks it apart.

A healthy business expands across the account base. A concentrated one looks identical from the outside. Until you separate the top accounts from everything else. One is growing. The other is quietly contracting.

You don't have a retention story. You have concentration masking decline.

The top expands on usage. The tail contracts on seats.

If you don't know the split, a buyer will.

#PrivateEquity #ValueCreation #ExitReadiness

Here's exactly where AI adds genuine value in a business workflow — and where it doesn't.

Where AI genuinely helps:
→ Classifying unstructured inputs (WhatsApp messages, emails, form responses) into structured categories 
→ Drafting first versions of repetitive written outputs (summaries, proposals, responses) 
→ Extracting specific data from messy documents (invoices, contracts, intake forms) 
→ Routing decisions that require reading intent, not just matching keywords → Summarising long threads or histories before a human needs to act

Where AI adds risk, not value:
→ Final decisions on anything with real consequences (payments, contracts, hiring) 
→ Customer-facing communications that need deep relationship context 
→ Processes that are still being defined — AI bakes in the wrong version 
→ Anything where the error cost is higher than the efficiency gain 
→ Replacing human judgment on complex, novel problems

The pattern:
AI is great at the first pass — interpreting, extracting, drafting, routing. Humans are better at the final judgment — deciding, relating, contextualising.

Build your AI layer to handle the first pass. 
Build your human layer to handle everything AI shouldn't touch.

That's the architecture that actually works.

We are seeking an entry-to-mid level QA professional to join the Quality Center of Excellence team in an AI-focused test design role. This role will support multiple project teams across the organization by using AI tools to accelerate and improve the creation of high-quality test cases, test scenarios, and coverage strategies. In addition to AI-led test design support, this person will help maintain quality assets in qTest and contribute to the long-term vision of integrating approved test cases into Tosca-based automation workflows.


Key Responsibilities

• Support project teams with AI-assisted test design based on user stories, requirements, functional specifications, technical specifications, future-state process documents, scenarios, and existing test assets.

• Act as an AI SME for assigned projects, owning AI-based test case generation and prompt refinement for those teams.
• Design and refine prompts for tools such as ChatGPT, Copilot, and other LLM-based assistants to generate relevant and executable test cases.

Required Qualifications and Core Skills

• 6+ years of experience in software testing, QA, or quality engineering.
• Basic working knowledge of software testing principles, test design techniques, test execution, defect tracking, test management tools, and quality assurance processes.
• Experience working in Agile/Scrum environments with sprints, user stories, and backlog-driven delivery.
• Working knowledge of prompt engineering for QA and test design, including the ability to create structured prompts that improve output quality, reduce iteration, and increase test coverage.
• Ability to analyze user stories and historical test assets from prior sprints in order to design better prompts and generate more accurate, reusable test cases.
• Familiarity with AI-assisted documentation and content generation using tools such as ChatGPT, Copilot, or similar generative AI solutions in a testing or business context.
• Hands-on comfort with test management tools and asset maintenance activities; exposure to qTest is strongly preferred.
• Exposure to or willingness to learn Tosca automation, Tricentis products, and automation-readiness concepts for manual test cases.
• Tricentis, Tosca, qTest, or other testing tool certifications are preferred where available.

The Hidden Revenue Leakage in Contract Lifecycle Management 

Many organisations buy CLM platforms expecting instant transformation, but software cannot fix broken ownership, conflicting incentives, or unmanaged commercial workflows.

Missed renewals destroy revenue 🚩
Pricing inconsistency erodes margin 🚩
Slow approvals delay cash 🚩
Weak controls increase risk 🚩
Manual reporting slows decisions 🚩

No automation layer. No risk detection logic. No alerting model. No control thresholds by design.  

If your systems don't share the same truth, the tool will only amplify confusion and support poor decisions.

Many firms chase 3–15% AI uplift in selected functions while ignoring far larger value leakage embedded in contracts, pricing, renewals, and obligations.

AI can create targeted gains, but many organisations overlook structural leakage in contract lifecycle management that can materially exceed any isolated AI benefits.

Contracts are not documents. 
They are the operating system of enterprise value. 

The biggest CLM opportunity is rarely the tool itself. It is redesigning the operating model architecture around revenue, control, risk, and accountability, while ensuring enterprise systems operate as one coherent platform rather than siloed entities with different realities.

If you see similarities in your current operating model, you already know this will not be solved by another tool or by adding more specialist headcount. 

Even worse is hiring people who sell AI hype or manual process management while leaving the underlying operating model and target state unchanged. This is not an enterprise built for scale.

This problem will be resolved through clear strategy, structural redesign, and disciplined execution.

The job market right now is competitive, selective, and fast‑changing.
Companies are hiring — but they are hiring carefully.
Roles are fewer, expectations are higher, and skills matter more than titles.

Here’s what I’m seeing across industries:
✅ Skills over degrees – Practical knowledge is winning
✅ Experience > keywords on resumes
✅ Contract & project roles are increasing
✅ AI, data, automation, and operations skills are in high demand
✅ Soft skills (communication, problem‑solving, ownership) matter more than ever

For job seekers, this means:

1.Keep upskilling
2.Customize your resume and LinkedIn profile
3.Focus on impact, not just responsibilities
4.Stay consistent and patient

✅ The market is tough — but it’s not closed ✅

Those who adapt, learn, and stay visible will win.

The AI Act comes up in almost every first meeting I have with prospects.

Most people have heard of it.
Most teams are still figuring out what it means in day-to-day hiring.

Good to see De Tijd covering this for a broader audience too.
De Tijd summarizes key rights for candidates. In our product team, we translate those into 4 design principles from day 1:
1. Respect hard boundaries
Some uses are simply not allowed, like emotion recognition in workplace contexts, except narrow legal exceptions.
2. Treat hiring workflows as high-risk by default
If AI screens, filters or ranks candidates, you need controls, logging, oversight, and clear usage boundaries.
3. Make transparency operational
Not a policy document nobody reads. Real in-product visibility on where AI is used and what it influences.
4. Make human oversight real
Not a checkbox. Recruiters need to review context and challenge outcomes, for example through call recordings or speech-to-text transcripts when relevant.

For us, compliance should not start in contract negotiations. It should already be built into the product from day one.

And 95% of builders are using exactly 2 of them.

1/ The model layer most people get wrong

Haiku for speed. Sonnet for balance. Opus for depth.
Using Opus for everything is like hiring a CFO to answer your emails.
Match the model to the task. Your bill drops 60%+ overnight.

2/ Skills and MCP are the real unlock

Skills let Claude remember how YOU work. Not generic prompts. Your decision frameworks, your scoring criteria, your voice.
MCP connects Claude to your actual stack. GHL, n8n, your CRM, your database.
This is not chatbot territory. This is an operating layer.

3/ The 48-hour build pipeline nobody talks about

Module 00: Score your idea across 5 dimensions before writing a line of code.
Module 01: Feed Claude 12 context files so it understands your market, your ICP, your positioning.
Module 02: Pick from 13 GTM strategies studied across 100+ companies.
Module 03-05: Claude Code builds the entire product. Not assists. Builds.
Module 06: Ship in 48 hours.

I tested this with MENA founders running 2-5 person teams.
The ones who set up context files first shipped 3x faster than the ones who jumped straight to code.

4/ What this means for small teams in the region

A founder in Dubai told me last month: "I canceled my Upwork contracts."
Not because the freelancers were bad.
Because Claude Code with proper Skills wrote better code than most contractors, and did it in minutes.

The gap between a solo founder with Claude and a funded 15-person team is shrinking every week.
If you are still treating AI as a chatbot, you are already behind.

The 3 HR vendors I saw at HR Tech Europe that NEED to evolve VS the 3 that are quietly winning...
(I'll get DMs for this - worth it)

I spent 2 days on the floor - getting demos, asking the questions buyers actually care about, + not the ones the booths wanted me to ask...

Here's what I saw 👇

NEEDS TO EVOLVE

1. HiBob

Platform feels overwhelming - just felt like features on features on features.
When I asked about new automations on their system, the example I got back was contract creation... which is something Adobe Sign has done for years.

I also checked our their ATS (that has aparently been out for 1 year already) - functionable, but quite basic, ie you can't drag & drop candidates the way you can in Ashby.
When I asked if their own team uses it, they said no... that was surprising.

2. Indeed

Impressive stand, 100% - but I didn't see many people actually staying to talk.
They've got an AI assistant for job seekers + hiring managers, but the platform itself looks unchanged.

The candidate database is genuinely insane - especially for roles that never get posted on LinkedIn.
But when people can X-ray search & use tools like Claude Code to source... why are we scrolling Indeed manually in 2026?

Massive presence. Underwhelming innovation.

3. Deel

They're stretching into everything - payroll, ATS, compliance, the lot. Payroll is still solid (it's their bread & butter & it shows).

But the ATS feels like a simplified Ashby that didn't quite stay simple. 
The interview feedback feature flags managers for "constructive criticism" - which sounds great in theory, but, in reality, managers are ALREADY swamped, they're probably going to ignore it...

When you try to do everything, it gets harder to know what you're actually known for.

WINNING

1. 50skills

Actually building AI agents (not chatbots). There's a difference & most teams haven't figured it out yet.
They plug into legacy systems you can't escape & empower HR users to build automations without needing eng. experience.

This is what "AI-native" actually looks like.

2. GloPros
The underdog of the event. What they've built in 18 months is impressive, & their CEO is refreshingly transparent about where they're headed (genuinely rare).
One to watch.

3. Shapes (notable mention)

Not at HR Tech (boo!), BUT - AI chatbot that actually delivers, automate any workflow, connects w/ Claude, the UI is stunning... & yes, that matters.

It makes you WANT to use it. 

---
The other thing I kept hearing from attendees:

people WANT a marketplace to compare HR tools side by side. Stack efficiency, cost, integrations, all in one place.
Surprised nothing like that exists yet.. 👀
---
Summary: The gap between AI-native & AI-slapped-on is getting wider every quarter. The bar is rising... fast.

(That's the standard we're trying to build no other choice.. against. Ask me in 8 weeks if we got it right 😎)

AI agencies are not the future of AI automation.
They are the most expensive way to get it wrong.
Here is what actually works.

You sign a contract. A sales person hands you to a project manager. The project manager briefs a strategist. The strategist briefs the person who actually builds the thing. That person opens Claude, Make, or n8n and gets to work.

Four people. One builder. You paid for all four.

The builder is not the problem. The structure is.

I have been on both sides of this. I know what the actual build work looks like. And I know what the markup looks like on top of it.

What a solo operator with the right stack delivers:

→ No handoff chain between you and the person building 
→ No "we need to loop in the team" delays 
→ No retainer to keep five people employed between your projects 
→ Direct communication, same day. Changes happen in the same session. 
→ One context window. One operator. Full accountability.

The numbers from my own work:

$2,000+/month saved per client. 85% reduction in manual processing time. 200+ workflows shipped. All of it solo, with Claude Code, Gemini, and a stack built over 2 years of production work.

Agencies are not evil. They make sense at a certain scale.

But most founders hiring AI agencies are not at that scale. They are paying enterprise prices for startup problems.

You do not need a team.
You need one person who has done this 200 times and knows exactly where to start.

What made you consider an agency over a solo operator for your last project?

Then the "Roulette" starts:

❌ You spend more time managing the vendor than managing your client.

It’s time to stop hiring vendors and start plugging into an engine.

1️⃣ No "spray and pray." We use AI to identify the intent, but human intelligence to start the conversation.

Every industrial revolution killed jobs. Everyone created new sectors that absorbed millions.
The AI revolution is different. And pretending otherwise is dangerous.
Here’s the pattern history taught us:

The steam engine wiped out hand-weavers → factories hired millions.
Electricity ended gas lighting → power grids employed entire generations.
Computers killed typing pools → software became a $5 trillion industry.
Each time, the new sector was big enough to absorb the displaced.

Now ask yourself honestly: Is AI doing the same?
Hiring has already slowed for entry-level programmers and analysts — the workers whose tasks AI is most adept at performing. The new AI roles being created — prompt engineers, model trainers, AI ethics officers — are real. But they’re high-skill, low-headcount. They don’t absorb millions.

This is the structural difference nobody in a boardroom wants to say out loud.
Previous revolutions replaced your hands.
This one is replacing your first draft- your analysis, your brief, your code review, your contract summary.
Entry-level jobs have greater exposure to AI, and generative AI adoption is already reducing entry-level hiring, especially for tasks that can be automated. The ladder that an entire generation climbed — junior role → mid-level → senior judgment — is losing its bottom rungs.

So what do you actually DO with this?
Stop asking, “Is my job safe?” Start asking “what can only I do?”
The roles that will thrive aren’t necessarily the ones hardest to automate. They’re the ones where humans are the product:
→ Care and connection — nursing, therapy, coaching. Society won’t outsource these to machines.
→ Physical complexity — electricians, surgeons, builders. The real world is still messy.
→ Trust-based relationships — the client who signs a $10M deal needs a human across the table.
→ AI governance — auditing, ethics, accountability. Roles in AI governance and ethics are expanding precisely because transparency and accountability require human judgment. 
→ Creative direction — not execution (AI handles that), but taste, vision, and the courage to make a call.
And here’s the data point worth holding onto: workers with AI skills now command a 56% wage premium — up from 25% just a year ago. 
The gap between those who direct AI and those it replaces is widening fast.

The industrial revolution didn’t ask permission. Neither will this one.
The question isn’t whether your role will change. It will.
The question is whether you’re spending more time using AI today than you are worrying about it.
Because the people building careers around this moment aren’t waiting for new sectors to appear.
They’re building them.

IT Staffing Is Quietly Coming Back. But It Looks Nothing Like Before.
Two years of frozen budgets. Stalled projects. A hiring market stuck in neutral.
Now? Things are finally moving again.
Activity is picking up. Projects that sat on hold are starting to move forward. But for many firms, revenue is still catching up improving activity without immediate financial relief is defining the IT staffing market right now. 

This is not the boom of 2021.
It's a year of rebalancing modest growth of ~2% to $183.3 billion, more predictable hiring cycles and efficiency driven gains. 

But here is what nobody's telling you.
The rules of the game have completely changed.
→ Degrees are out. Skills are in. 92% of employers are now open to hiring candidates who gained skills outside a degree. Skills-first hiring can expand talent pools nearly tenfold. Your GitHub profile is your new resume.

→ Niche > Generalist. Every time. There is been a tectonic shift away from software engineering, it now makes up only 10% of in demand roles. Cloud engineering, data analytics, AI and machine learning are rising to the top.

→ Speed is the new competitive advantage. The 2026 IT staffing market rewards buyers who make fewer, faster, better priced decisions, and punishes the old pattern of a big vendor list and a slow comp calibration cycle. 

→ Specialized talent moves in days, not weeks. Candidates with verified skills in MLOps, Prompt Engineering, Cloud FinOps and Cybersecurity? They are off the market in under 14 days. Move slow. Lose them.
→ AI is running the front door. 84% of hiring processes now employ AI. If your resume isn't optimized for it, you are not even getting to a human.

→ Contract-to-hire is having its moment. Low risk. Real evaluation. Both sides get to decide. It's the format smart teams are using to navigate uncertainty right now.
The companies winning the talent war in 2026 are not the ones with the biggest job boards.
They are the ones who moved fast, dropped outdated requirements and treated candidates like customers.
87% of tech leaders are optimistic about 2026. 61% are planning headcount growth. Yet 65% say finding qualified talent is harder than a year ago.

Optimism + urgency + a talent crunch = a very interesting market.
Are you hiring right now? What's your biggest challenge, finding talent or closing them fast?
Drop it below. 👇

AI agents generate 80% of the solution fast. The remaining 20% is where projects break.

Addy Osmani calls it the 80% problem. A METR study found developers using AI actually took 19% longer on real tasks — while believing they were 20% faster. And PR review time jumped 91% in teams with heavy AI adoption.

The bottleneck isn't the model. It's the architecture around it.

In the new newsletter, I walk through what changes when you treat architecture, not prompting, as the real lever:

→ Why spec-driven development (70% problem definition, 30% execution) inverts how most people use AI today
→ The shift from vibe coding to agentic engineering, and what Karpathy's 80/20 flip means in practice
→ How a serverless architecture with clear contracts and scoped roles becomes the best "prompt" for AI agents
→ The data on AI code quality: 1.75x more logic errors, 1.57x more security findings
→ Why junior developer hiring dropped 25% at top tech firms while seniors ship 2.5x more AI-generated code

The engineers winning with AI aren't the ones with the best prompts. They're the ones with the best architecture.

Today is the day. 

Resumye.com is live!! Proud and excited to introduce India’s voice-first resume builder and job marketplace. 🎉

Six months. Zero shortcuts. One mission. Today I’m soft-launching Resumye.com — and I genuinely believe it’s going to change how 12 million Indians find work.

Here’s the problem we’re solving:
→ India has 12 million gig workers today. NITI Aayog projects 23.5 million by 2030.
→ 40% earn under ₹15,000/month.
→ National literacy is 76%. English proficiency is far lower.
→ Yet every job portal in India still demands typed resumes in English.

The result: a delivery rider in Patna, an electrician in Hubballi, a forklift operator in Bhiwandi — all locked out of the very platforms built to hire them.

I rebuilt the entire flow around how they actually live: phone in hand, voice as input, mother tongue as default.

What’s live in the soft launch:
🎤 60-second voice resume (no typing required)
🌐 12 languages — Hindi, Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Marathi, Bengali, Gujarati, Odia, Punjabi, Malayalam, Urdu, English
📍 PIN-code-based “jobs near me” matching
✅ Skill badges with proof verification (FSSAI, driving licence, ITI, PSARA)
📅 Availability calendar — workers mark free days, employers filter by them. No more no-shows.
⭐ Trust Score built from real employer ratings
📹 Async video answers — candidates reply on their own time

🎯 Employer side: AI job description generator, candidate search, video briefs, interview scheduling, offer letters, group hiring for squads

💳 Pay-per-use unlocks. No bloated SaaS contracts.
API Integration for enterprises.

Why this matters now:
Gig is 2% of the workforce, projected to hit 6.7% by 2029-30 — ₹2.35 lakh crore of GDP. 
The hiring infrastructure to match it does not exist yet.
We’re building it.

To VCs — we’d love to walk you through traction and roadmap.
To hiring managers — early access for warehouse, logistics, retail, hospitality, construction, and delivery teams. DMs open.
To everyone else — watch this space. There’s more coming.

🇮🇳 Built in India. Providing digital access to the gig and blue collar workers.

If your AI vendor needs a 3-year contract to keep you... what does that tell you?

ICONIQ's State of GTM 2026 surveyed 150+ B2B executives. The data is hard to ignore. One-year deals jumped to 38% from 2023. Over half of all new contracts are now a year or less.

Read that again. The market has spoken.

A VP of Sales at a $100M+ ARR startup summed it up — "Everyone wants to buy. They just aren't sure what they want in AI next year. So nobody signs long-term."

Most founders are panicking. How do we lock in revenue?

Ha... maybe ask a different question? Why are your customers afraid to commit?

Because most AI tools are wrappers. The platform changes every few months. What's impressive in January is commoditized by June. Customers know this. 

The data proves they know this.

Here's my take. If your product delivers outcomes or solutions, not features, you don't need a lock-in. Your results are the lock-in.

At MyNextHire, we don't chase multi-year contracts. We chase multi-year impact. If hiring got measurably better... why would you leave?

It's not "how do I lock them in." It's "why would they stay?"

Are you building something people are locked into... or something they'd choose again tomorrow?

Agentic AI in HR: The A–Z Leaders Can’t Ignore

HR is entering the agent era.

Agentic AI is not an upgrade. It is a shift in how HR operates.

Most HR teams still use AI as a tool. Leaders who move ahead are building systems where AI executes, decides, and improves workflows continuously.

Fragmented tools, manual workflows, and reactive decisions limit HR’s ability to scale impact.

Move from tools to an integrated AI operating model across the HR stack.
Body:

A–Z of Agentic AI in HR:

A — Autonomous Agents
Execute workflows across systems, not just respond.

B — Behavioral Intelligence
Use psychometric and behavioral data for better hiring decisions.

C — Cognitive Search
Create a single access layer across policies, data, and systems.

D — Decision Intelligence
Shift from reporting to AI-recommended actions.

E — Experience Orchestration
Design employee journeys that adapt in real time.

F — Foundation Models
Base intelligence powering copilots and agents.

G — Graph-Based Talent Intelligence
Map skills, roles, and career paths dynamically.

H — Hyperautomation
Automate end-to-end HR workflows.

I — Intelligent Document Processing
Extract and process resumes, contracts, and forms automatically.

J — Job Architecture Intelligence
Build real-time skill-based role structures.

K — Knowledge Graphs
Connect employee data, skills, and relationships.

L — LLMOps
Deploy, monitor, and scale AI in production.

M — Multi-Agent Orchestration
Multiple agents collaborating across workflows.

N — Natural Language Analytics
Query HR data conversationally.

O — Operational AI Layer
Embed AI directly into HR operations.

P — Predictive Workforce Modeling
Forecast attrition and workforce needs.

Q — Query Engines
Unify access across fragmented HR systems.

R — Responsible AI
Ensure governance, fairness, and explainability.

S — Skills Intelligence Platforms
Continuously map and update workforce skills.

T — Talent Marketplace
Enable internal mobility through AI-driven matching.

U — Unstructured Data Intelligence
Analyze surveys, emails, and feedback at scale.

V — Vector Databases
Enable memory and semantic search for HR systems.

W — Workforce Digital Twins
Simulate workforce scenarios before decisions.

X — Explainable AI
Make AI decisions transparent and auditable.

Y — Your AI Stack
Integrate systems into a scalable ecosystem.

Z — Zero-Touch HR
Autonomous, AI-led HR operations.

Agentic AI is not a feature.
It is the new operating model for HR.

Leaders who understand this will not just improve HR.
They will redefine how it operates.

What does a missed call actually cost a plumbing business?

We surveyed small plumbing operators and here's what we found: the average job value for a new residential customer is between €180–€400. The average plumber misses 4–7 calls per day while actively working. That's 120–210 missed call opportunities per month.

Even at a 25% conversion rate, that's 30–52 potential new jobs per month being handed to competitors — simply because nobody answered the phone.

The solution most people consider? Hiring a receptionist. Cost: €1,200–€1,800/month minimum, plus insurance, sick days, and management time.

The solution most people don't yet know about? IntellentVoice. An AI voice assistant that answers every call in under 2 seconds, books jobs, and costs a fraction of a human hire.

€0.05/minute pay-as-you-go. Or go Pro at €29/month with 500 minutes included.

No contracts. No surprises. No missed calls.

intellentvoice.com

We have an exciting contract opportunity with one of our reputed client (Payroll: MSys Inc.) for a GenAI Copilot / AI-ML Engineer role.
If you have strong experience in GenAI, RAG, and Agentic AI, this could be a great fit!

📌 Role Details

🔹 Position: GenAI Copilot / AI-ML Engineer
🔹 Experience: 8+ Years
🔹 Employment Type: Contract
🔹 Location: Remote

🚀 Key Responsibilities

✔️ Build and deploy end-to-end LLM-based GenAI applications
✔️ Work with LLMs like OpenAI / Azure OpenAI, Gemini, Claude, LLaMA, Mistral
✔️ Design Agentic & multi-agent systems (MCP / A2A)
✔️ Develop RAG pipelines and document intelligence solutions
✔️ Use frameworks like LangChain, LangGraph, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, AutoGen, Google ADK, MS Copilot
✔️ Build scalable backend services using FastAPI / Flask
✔️ Develop AI chatbots, copilots & dashboards
✔️ Deploy & optimize AI systems on cloud platforms
✔️ Ensure Responsible AI, security & scalability

🧠 Required Skills

🔹 Strong Python (FastAPI, Flask)
🔹 ML, Deep Learning, NLP & GenAI fundamentals
🔹 LLMs, embeddings & prompt engineering
🔹 Agentic AI (MCP / A2A)
🔹 RAG architectures & vector DBs (FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus)
🔹 Frontend basics (HTML, CSS, JS, Streamlit; React preferred)
🔹 API development (REST / WebSockets)
🔹 MLOps & Cloud (AWS / Azure / GCP, Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD, MLflow / SageMaker / Vertex AI)

⭐ Good to Have

✨ LLM fine-tuning (LoRA, PEFT, Hugging Face)
✨ Autonomous agents & async workflows
✨ RPA + Agentic AI integration
✨ AI governance, monitoring & security

📢 Interested?
Kindly review the JD and share your updated CV to mahesh@msysinc.com

⸻

* Location: Remote
* Employment Type: Contract / Full-Time

* Define and implement Power Platform & Copilot Studio governance frameworks

* Power Platform Admin Center

⸻

* DevOps & CI/CD pipelines
* Prompt Engineering & GenAI controls

⸻

* Experience driving enterprise AI adoption
* Hands-on with security, compliance, and scalable platform design

⸻
